title: Why debt obsession grows in the dark
subtitle: The avoidance-rumination cycle: why not looking makes the number louder.
excerpt: A short reading on why debt thinking is loudest when the debt isn't being actively addressed. The avoidance-rumination cycle explained: the mind returns to what it hasn't resolved, and avoidance keeps the number unresolved. Useful context before working with any of the debt-obsession practices.
sourceNotes: The avoidance-rumination cycle is documented across cognitive-behavioural therapy literature and anxiety research. The specific money-avoidance pattern is described in Day 6 of MONEY: A 12-Week Tapping Program (Rebecca J Page, 2025), "Stop obsessing over debt."

content.text: Debt obsession has a particular quality: the thinking is loudest not when you are sitting down to deal with the debt, but in the middle of doing something else. In a meeting. In the supermarket. Just before sleep. The number arrives uninvited and replays until the next distraction.

The mind returns to what it hasn't resolved. When a problem has been looked at, assessed, and given a response (even a partial one), the mind can file it temporarily. When a problem has been avoided, the mind keeps it open. It returns to it on a loop because the loop is the only thing it can do with an unresolved item.

Debt fits this pattern particularly well. The shame and anxiety attached to debt make direct contact with the numbers uncomfortable, so avoidance is the natural response: don't open the statement, don't log into the account, don't look. But the avoidance keeps the debt unresolved in the mind's filing system. The loop continues.
Why avoidance makes the number louder

There is a specific paradox in debt avoidance: the number that the avoidance is trying to escape from gets louder, not quieter, the longer the avoidance continues. The mind with an unresolved item runs the item on a background loop. The loop keeps the item present even when the banking app is closed.

A brief, deliberate look at the actual number (one minute, eyes on the balance, then close the app) gives the mind something concrete to file. It interrupts the loop. The number after the look is the same as the number before. What changes is the mind's relationship to it: looked at rather than avoided.
The obsession loop versus the planning session

The practices in this library address the obsession loop (the involuntary replaying of the number), not the debt itself. Tapping, energy statements, and journal prompts shift the emotional charge around the debt. They are not repayment plans, and they don't replace a repayment plan. The planning session is a separate task, done at a separate time, ideally after the obsession loop has been disrupted enough that the numbers can be looked at without the charge.

If you are using the debt practices to avoid the planning session as well as to reduce the obsession, notice that distinction. The goal is neutrality: a state in which the balance can be looked at steadily, rather than distance from the numbers.

The tapping practice addresses the obsession loop directly. The energy statement offers a release and allow for the same pattern. The journal prompt asks what story the debt is carrying, often the story is heavier than the number. The affirmation gives a one-sentence counter to say before opening the app.

The avoidance-rumination cycle is well documented in cognitive-behavioural therapy and in anxiety research. The specific debt-avoidance pattern here is adapted from Day 6 of MONEY: A 12-Week Tapping Program (Rebecca J Page, 2025). The day looks at debt obsession as a pattern that tapping can break.
